title: Parameterized complexity of Grundy Coloring
statement_text: Is \textsc{Grundy Coloring} fixed-parameter tractable, i.e., solvable in time $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$, when parameterized by $k$ (the Grundy number)? Alternatively, does it admit an algorithm running in time $n^{k^{O(1)}}$?
context_text: Zaker observed in 2006 that a minimal $k$-witness has at most $2^{k-1}$ vertices, yielding a brute-force $f(k) \cdot n^{2^{k-1}}$ XP algorithm. Whether an FPT algorithm (or even a significantly sub-exponential XP algorithm) exists was raised as an open question in multiple papers over the following decade.
attributed_to: raised in multiple prior works (refs [37, 26, 20, 4] in the paper)
paper_contribution: Proves that Grundy Coloring is W[1]-complete and that no $f(k) \cdot n^{o(2^{k-\log k})}$ algorithm exists under the ETH, settling both the FPT question and the weaker $n^{k^{O(1)}}$ question in the negative.
notes: PDF source; time-bound exponents reconstructed from garbled PDF text (e.g. 'f(k)n2k−1' interpreted as $f(k)\cdot n^{2^{k-1}}$, 'no(2k−log k)' as $n^{o(2^{k-\log k})}$).

title: Parameterized complexity of b-Chromatic Core (Panolan et al.)
statement_text: Is \textsc{b-Chromatic Core}, parameterized by $k$ (the order of the desired core), W[1]-hard or fixed-parameter tractable?
context_text: Panolan et al. studied the b-chromatic core in relation to b-coloring and explicitly left as an open question whether b-Chromatic Core is W[1]-hard or FPT. The b-chromatic number problem was already known to be W[1]-hard, but no polytime algorithm was known even for constant $k$.
paper_contribution: Proves that b-Chromatic Core is W[1]-complete using a construction based on half-graphs, fully settling the open question.
notes: Attributed explicitly as 'Panolan et al. [JCSS '17]' (reference [36]) in both the abstract and the introduction.

title: FPT on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs for Grundy Coloring and b-Chromatic Core
statement_text: Are \textsc{Grundy Coloring} and \textsc{b-Chromatic Core} fixed-parameter tractable on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs when parameterized by $k$?
context_text: The W[1]-hardness constructions for both problems rely heavily on large half-graphs as color-propagation units; excluding large half-graphs may restore tractability. However, the key combinatorial lemma that gives FPT on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs collapses on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs, so the question is explicitly left open.
notes: Stated as an explicit open problem in the 'Limits and further questions' section; no formal numbered theorem environment.

title: FPT on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs for Grundy Coloring
statement_text: Does \textsc{Grundy Coloring} admit a fixed-parameter tractable algorithm on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs when parameterized by $k$?
context_text: The paper establishes FPT algorithms for b-Chromatic Core and Partial Grundy Coloring on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs (parameterized by $k+t$), but the analogous question for Grundy Coloring on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs is explicitly left unsettled.
notes: Stated explicitly as unsettled in the 'Limits and further questions' section; no formal numbered theorem environment.

title: Parameterized complexity of Partial Grundy Coloring on general graphs
statement_text: What is the parameterized complexity of \textsc{Partial Grundy Coloring} on general graphs, parameterized by $k$?
context_text: The authors were unable to prove W[1]-hardness for Partial Grundy Coloring because half-graphs cannot serve as color-propagation units (the partial Grundy number is already large on a large half-graph $H_{t,t}$). The class of $H_{t,t}$-free graphs is suggested as a promising intermediate case for understanding the general-graph complexity.
notes: Stated as an open problem in the 'Limits and further questions' section; no formal numbered theorem environment.
